speaker: interviewer
text: Tape for November 7, 1995. We're speaking with Mrs. Aranka [? Rosner ?] Siegal. Mrs. Siegal, you were describing the selection, and your sister was being shaved by prisoners, male prisoners.

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish male prisoners, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: And instead of coming back for our clothes, as they promised, from the showers, we never did come back to the bath house. The showers were outdoors. And after we came out of the showers, they doused our hair with like kerosene. And here we had all these little nicks and cuts on our scalp from those little machine guns-- I mean machine--

speaker: interviewer
text: Razors?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Hand machine-- I guess-- clippers. Clippers. And because they were made to cut fast, fast, fast. There were so many of us that had arrived. And they had to work very fest so they had nicked us in many places. And, oh, it was uneven. It was just the ugliest thing in the entire world. I [? tell ?] the children when I speak at schools, you know, how upset they get when they get a bad haircut. And can they picture how we felt, we were their age, you know, have these clippers go over our heads and leaving these uneven row and little nicks and blood and all that. And then we get this kerosene, and it's running into our eyes, you know. And it was just a total misery. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And instead of our own clothes, they handed us each one of these garments that had three holes-- two for the arms, one for the head. And they all look the same, like gray sacks. Maybe that's why I hate gray. I've always hated gray. And I never really thought of it until just now. But we were all like gray pelicans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not stripes?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. We all looked the same-- just solid gray, solid gray. And the stripes were for men, and some of the women who were a certain work detail. But the common people like ourselves, who just arrived, and they didn't decide what they do with us, this was our garment. Nothing under it of course. And a pair of wooden shoes-- and we each had like two wooden shoes thrown at us. It could have been two lefts two rights. We tried to straighten it out between ourselves. And my sister was yelling my name again. And I was always being called, it seems. And I stuck so close to her in the shower, out of the shower. I didn't let her out of my sight, because having lost Mother, she was all I had. So I was right there, next to her, touching her. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And she's calling my name. And I said, I'm here, right here. And she looked at me. And it was obvious she didn't recognize me. Without my hair, I had this-- all this dark long hair. Then she realized that she must look the same. So she walked over to the bathhouse. And it had tiny little windows. And I see her in front of the window, making all these crazy motions. I got scared. And I said, what are you doing? She says, I'm trying to figure out which one is me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because she's realized now, that we all look the same. And she just wanted to which one was her. Well, she looks really worse than I did, because I always had a full face. And she had sort of a narrow face. And without the hair, she really just didn't look like herself. But I stuck so close that I knew it was her. But we could not recognize ourselves. And then quickly, they had poured buckets full of liquids, and steaming liquids. And they gave us each a little cup, which they told us we will have to save for our internment. We have to have these metal-- metal cups always on us. And they gave us some liquid that was kind of bitter and greenish. Some kind of tea, no doubt.

speaker: interviewee
text: But a few minutes after we drank it, everybody started to laugh. And they were laughing at each other. And they were laughing about how funny everything was. And one of the nurses who was among us said, oh my god. They drugged us. She says, they must have put drugs in our tea. And that's exactly what they did to calm us down, because of what was coming, you know? Because they walked us back by the same railroad tracks that we came from. We had to go that way. And what was so amazing, that we looked around, the trees were gone. And all these people that we had left there was gone. Everything was gone. The place looked like it had absolutely been swept clean. It made you feel like you were crazy, like none of this ever happened, like you just imagined it, you know? Because there was not a sign, not a trace, not a rag, nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. Piles of glasses we saw, too, before we left. Because they kept taking off the glasses from people who had glasses on, and throwing it in a pile. And everything-- but everything was gone. And then they marched us to this barrack that had these double-decker beds, only they were nothing but boards, and no blankets, no nothing. And that's where we were to stay, in these barracks. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there piles of anything else, other than the eyeglasses?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not at that time. Not at the time. They didn't get them undressed. And their bundles, had-- they had all piled someplace in the back of the train. I haven't seen them, because somebody was talking about bundles-- that some people did have some little bundles with them. And that they had thrown them all in one pile in the back. And some of the women were looking to see if those were still there, if they could, you know, get some of it back or whatever. But no. Everything was gone. I really felt like they had taken some brooms and just swept everything away. It was just unbelievably like nothing happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they marched you to the barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: They marched us to the barracks. And these barracks were inside electric-- electrified wires. So they right away, this [INAUDIBLE] who walked us down, who was going to be in charge of us, who was a Polish woman, immediately told us that these wires are electrified. And if any of us, you know, touch it, we would be electrocuted. So don't try to run out or do anything stupid, because you'll be electrocuted on the spot. The other thing, I looked around and I saw all these lookout towers with the machine guns. And she explained that that was another thing. If anybody runs out of line or anything, that we would be gunned down. And that we would have to stay in the barracks, except when she tells us to come out. Every morning, they woke us up at daybreak. It was still dark outside. And we would have to line up five abreast. This became the thing-- five abreast every morning, and they called it the cell appel. And we'd be there shivering, you know, because we had this one single garment on us with nothing underneath it. And we were just so cold and so empty. Our stomachs was so hungry. We had nothing to eat that night, or the day in the cattle cart, or the day before, really. Nothing till the end of that day, towards the afternoon, they finally gave us some soup and some bread. But up to that time, it was all being counted, being sorted, and giving-- given all these instructions and everything. And we were given dog tags with our numbers on.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't have a-- did you have a tattoo?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I never got a tattoo. Some people were later on tattooed. Not right away. But when our barrack was being taken to be tattooed, my sister got very scared that it's another selection, because they had selections every day. People disappeared every day on these selections. So she was afraid that it was a selection. And she made me go with her to one of the barracks and hide out. And when the people from our barrack came back, this was after we were there for a couple of weeks and we had some hair growth. They had a new clipping. And they had a clean uniform. And they had numbers. We had no numbers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the uniform gray?

speaker: interviewee
text: Same gray uniform. And we thought, well, now we'll really get in trouble, because we have some hair, and we don't have numbers. And our uniform is dirty. But the reason they had done all of this is because they were taking us out, on a work detail, taking us out from here. And they put us into a work detail to build a road out of rocks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was--

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had to lug these rocks, where there were guys that were breaking the rocks up with picks. And then we had to pick up these rocks and carry-- carry them to a certain place where they were building a road out of rocks. And if you didn't pick up a-- a rock large enough to suit them, or you weren't moving fast enough, the guards beat you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And now, some of these guards were political prisoners. They weren't Germans. But you know, they seemed to be just as mean. Like for instance, the woman who was in charge of us, that [INAUDIBLE], when we complained about anything, she said, oh, you think you have it tough? She said, you should have seen it when we came here years ago. We didn't even have a roof over our head. We built these barracks. So you are lucky you have a roof over your head. And also, during separation-- oh, we didn't consider them being part of us, even though they were prisoners, because they were in charge of us and treated us, you know, like if we were just dirt, because they were our bosses. And they got better food, better quarters. They lived in the barrack, but they had like a whole little partition just to themselves. And they had a little stove. We could smell them cooking in there, when we were so starving, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, this woman was so cruel, that when we first were separated from our parents, we had asked, you know, when we would be reunited. And they were saying, even as we were being separated, don't fret because it's very temporary. On Sunday, you will all be reunited with your families. You're just being separated for work.

speaker: interviewee
text: So Sunday came around, and it was already almost nighttime, and my sister-- my sister-- my friend Joli and I-- my sister was off talking to other prisoners, trying to find some information on my grandmother and my sister Roszi because we had found out before we left the ghetto that they had left earlier. Some of the people that had seen the train go through our town, some of the Hungarian trainsmen who knew us and got messages from them, bore the messages to the ghetto to tell us that my mother's mother Bubbe and Roszi had been taken away. So we knew that they got there earlier. So she was looking for some people from that village so she could find out some news them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my sister, Joli, and I were sitting, crying. And this woman, the [INAUDIBLE] came over to us and she said, oh. I see I have two crybabies over here, she said. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What nationality was she?

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish. But it had nothing to do with nationality. They were so inbred by the Germans by now, you know, that they were acting just like them. And she said, so what are you crying about? And I was afraid to speak up. But my sister Joli said well, you know it's Sunday. And they had promised us that on Sunday we would be reunited with our mothers, and it's almost dark.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she said, your mothers. Oh. Is that what you're crying about, she said. Look at those smokestacks, she says. What do you think they're doing in those buildings? Do you think they're breaking bread for you in there? Do you think they're bakeries? Just like this, sarcastic as anything. She said, those are crematoriums. And of course we already heard, but we didn't listen. We didn't believe. We couldn't. She said, that's where they are. That's where they took them, straight from the train. And if you don't stop crying, that's what will happen to your too.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well I stopped crying. I think I stopped crying for good. But Joli got very, very angry. And she said, you know if that is true, and I have been very suspicious of that smell, it does smell like flesh and bone. And she said, if that's really true, she said, I'm not gonna live. I don't want to. If this is what the world is really like, and the other people don't care, and they aren't doing anything about it, they just let this happen, she says, then there's no reason to go on. I'm just not gonna live. Well, of course my sister and I really tried to talk her out of it, because her mother and my mother was best of friends. As a matter of fact, during the war, because her father and my father were in the same work camp, they had become so close they shared their mail. When one of them got a postcard, they would run, tell the other one. Because mail was very, very scarce. And they were just little open postcards. And they could say very little. Everything was censored. But still it was a sign that they're still alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: So because of our mother's closeness, and of course, I loved her very much. She was like another sister. I begged her, Joli, don't do anything foolish. But when they picked us to work, she put up a fight. She wouldn't go to work. When they brought us food, she wouldn't eat her food. And she was just very, very defiant.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we would really try to literally drag her with us when we were going to work. Even Mrs. Hollander, the lady we lined up with, and her two daughters, they all tried to get Joli to go with us. Well, this one day, we couldn't get her to go. And the [INAUDIBLE] said, leave her. When we came back, Joli was gone. Joli died before her 15th birthday. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That was the end of my friend, Joli. In my books, I talk about her. And even about the book I'm writing now, where it's not about their experience. It's not about me. I named one of the characters Joli just so I would keep her going. But she died in Auschwitz because she wouldn't cooperate, and she didn't want to live really.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- when you went on the work details, was that far from the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We had to go outside the fence, outside the gate.

speaker: interviewer
text: You walked?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They walked us, and then they walked us back. And we would have something to eat before we left, some tea and a piece of bread, that had to last us the day and some soup. And they brought us back at night.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did the local people see you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. This-- this camp was so far away from local people that, I mean, their houses weren't too far, to some of the people who went out of the camp to work, like a lot of them did. We didn't. And they actually walked out of the camp. We just walked out from our particular compound, which was Camp B, brought into another camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: But if you walk actually out of these camps, on the other side of the railroad track, they saw the civilians and they saw the houses. And they would come back and tell us, you know, that these people don't even see them. They don't look at them. And they beg for food or bread or whatever. And they just are completely ignored. And they told us they were Polish people, because we didn't even know we were actually, because, you know, we arrived there. And we were immersed in this atmosphere of smoke, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we were standing for our cell appel in the morning, there were such huge ashes falling and floating in the air, that they would land on us. And of course we had bare arms. And we tried to brush them off and it would just smear because it would be full of grease. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it all came from the crematorium. And we saw those crematoriums going night and day-- the red flames and the black smoke. Night and day, they kept them going. And the trains kept coming. And I remember one particular train arriving. We later found them-- found out that those people were coming from Vienna. And some of the women got out of the train, and it was a very sunny day, and they open parasols. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were very beautifully dressed. And we found out later on that some of these were people who had converted and some where half-Christian or only had one Christian-- I mean, Jewish grandparent, or parents, and that they were very privileged. But towards the end, because now this was the summer of 1944, they brought in all the ones that thought that, you know, by converting they would be treated specially. Well they were for-- they were for a while, but then they brought them in, and I remember when I saw those parasols, I just absolutely couldn't believe it. And I thought, just wait till you see what's waiting for you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you laugh?

speaker: interviewee
text: Laugh? No, no. I felt very sorry for them. And at the end, there was no room anymore. They took a lot of them just straight to the gas chambers.

speaker: interviewer
text: You say some of them were treated in a privileged way in the beginning.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the camp they were treated privileged?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. Before they brought them in. Before they brought them in. No. In the camps that I was in, in Auschwitz and Birkenau, I didn't see any of the Jews being treated any different than any of the others, except that some of them were put in a work detail that was special, because they were sorting the bundles and the clothing of the people who came in. And they would find some food there, you know? And these women all wore the striped uniforms and little white like handkerchiefs on their heads, little white kerchiefs. And they called them the white kerchiefs. That's what they called them. And one of my mother's friends, Mrs. Berger was in this detail. And the day that they were taking us when there was a selection, and they picked my sister to leave Auschwitz, to go to a camp to work outside of Auschwitz, into Germany. But of course we never knew where we were going. But these manufacturers came and made a selection. And they picked my sister, but they didn't pick me. And I was over at the side crying hysterically. Because we were separated. I tried always not to cry, but separation is something you cannot possibly hold yourself together or keep yourself in-- in control.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [DRINKS] I was crying because now the last person that I had was taken away from me. Because she was put into this line of five. They were lining them all up, that they had selected. And I was just kind of pushed to the side with the discards. And I knew that where they pushed me was not a good place, because I looked around and you could always tell that the people who looked the worst were going to the gas chambers, you know? And the people who looked the best, they were being picked for work. And don't forget, I had lied about my age. So now I was about 14 years old. And I had lost probably-- and I was always a skinny, scrawny kid as you see on my pictures. But being under these conditions with so little food and everything, I must have by then lost 15 pounds. So if I came in there weighing 90 pounds, you know, and I lost 15, I was like 75 pounds. And what-- when was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was September of 1944. September of 1944. It was right around the Jewish holidays, because people were talking about the holidays. There were people who kept calendars under their bunk and the dirt with a stick. Because one day, my sister came over, and she gave me a piece of her bread. And I said, what is this for? And she always tried to give me some of her food, because I was younger, and she said she promised mother to take care of me, et cetera, so she was always looking after me. But this was like a whole portion of the bread. And I said, what is this for? She said, it's your birthday. You're 14. I said, how do you know? She said, some women are keeping a calendar. It's June 10th. But then, in September, we-- they were talking about the Jewish holidays coming. And that's when the selection took place.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, I stood there crying with all of the others. And suddenly, as by a miracle, this friend of my mother's, Mrs. Berger, comes by, with her white kerchief. They had just come through the gate. They're making these people marking-- march out the gates, that were selected for work. And Mrs. Berger's work people, you know, her detail, work detail, is coming in the gate. So this is going out. She's coming in.

speaker: interviewee
text: As she sees me there, crying on the inside of the gate, and she says, what's the matter? And I said, they took my sister. She's over there. And you know, so quick-- I have never seen anything move as quick as she moved. She put down something she was holding. And she immediately took my arm, and she just flung me into that line. And she pulled somebody out of there, and made them go where I was. I mean, she just switched two people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Real quick fast-- my sister was already out the gate-- so I had to work my way to the other people,

speaker: interviewer
text: To get--

speaker: interviewee
text: Till-- till I get to her, and we sort of evened out the lines so we'd be five. Because they kept counting and counting and counting as we got through the gate, and then before they boarded us on the train. And Mrs. Berger even had a little package of food for us, which she had very often did, that she had given us, you know, as we were leaving. And instead of being happy like everybody, to leave this godforsaken place, with all the ashes and everything that was going on there, those trains of people being murdered constantly-- I start crying at the bathhouse, where they're giving us a bath before we leave in clean clothes. And another shave-- and they say that the shave was just to keep us from having lice and vermin, they called it. But we found out that was also so we couldn't run away-- not that you could run anywhere. But, I mean, with a shaved head, they would have picked you up immediately.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm crying. And my sister said to me, why are you crying? Everybody is happy to be leaving here. And I said, we're leaving Mother. I said, I'll never see her again. We're leaving her. She didn't want to say mother is dead, but she was trying to tell me, you know, that she would want us to leave, and let's go and so forth. But I cried on the train. They took us in a train. I didn't want to leave that place. And then they took us to an ammunition factory. Where? In Christianstadt. And in Christianstadt, the manufacturers came again for a selection. And they're selecting the strong ones. And they leave me behind. Even my sister was left behind. They wouldn't take her to work in their factories. And about five other people. So what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: So we just for a while, roamed around. This was at the-- a deserted summer camp, like a Boy Scout camp. In Christianstadt, they took over for us. And they had outdoor showers. And I remember taking like 10 showers a day, because, you know, all that time I didn't have a shower. I was making up for showers. We had no soap or anything, but I just kept going in the shower. And washed our clothes again, you know, and so forth, because we were there a few days by that time. And one day, we were out, reaching through the fence, which was not electrified, picking berries. And there was this whistle. They were calling us. You know, we knew this whistle already. There was a different siren for getting up in the morning, cell appel. And then there was a whistle when they were rounding you up for something special.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the few of us came running. I think maybe we were seven all together. And there were the two German women who were SS women and who were in charge of our camp. I only know their first names. Let's see if I remember it. Marta was the one that was really in charge, the big one. And she was quite a big woman-- always laughing. It was unbelievable. But she always had a smile on her face.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Gerta was the little one that never smiled. And then there was Lucy, who was medium-- very, very fair like my sister, blue-eyed. And she was actually a giggler. These women were unreal. They only acted serious when some of the men were around, the SS men, who were the guards around the camp. They had these lookout towers and the men were in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she asked, does anybody speak German? Does any of you speak German? Every hand went up, because we volunteered for anything and everything, because the one thing we found out, if you don't work, you die. So anything they asked us for, we volunteered. So then she asked that-- all the hands went up.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my German was very little, and some of the other people's German was really Yiddish. But we all volunteered. So they laughed and giggled, because they realized, you know, we didn't really speak German. So-- and my sister said that I flashed my biggest smile to them. I just gave them a big smile, you know, like pick me. And she said that she just couldn't believe it. Here I was smiling at these three German women-- in SS uniform. And sure enough, they picked me to come work in the kitchen, which is partially responsible-- a good-- good part of the stay over there is part of-- you know, responsible for my survival, because I had more food in the kitchen than the people that went to work. Besides I wouldn't have lasted a month, because in the dead of winter, they had to walk miles, like eight, 10 miles to work for the factories, depending which factories they were in. And they would come back every night, looking just like aluminium robot. Their entire face was aluminum. All you could see is just the inside of their eyes and the inside of their mouth. Everything else was covered in aluminum. The aluminum dust covered them completely. And there was no way to even wash it off.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your sister was with--

speaker: interviewee
text: It just didn't come off, that stuff. It got into their pores and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your sister part of those workers?

speaker: interviewee
text: [DRINKS] No. We were both very fortunate. We both went to work in the kitchen. She worked in the part of the kitchen which they called the [INAUDIBLE], where they peel the vegetables for the Germans's meal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
